  5. Common Reasons for China Trademark Rejection

Common Reasons for China Trademark Rejection

﻿# Common Reasons for China Trademark Rejection

Published: June 11, 2026 | Category: Guide | Read Time: 12 min | Tags: trademark-rejection, CNIPA, rejection-reasons, brand-protection, China-trademark

📋 Key Takeaway

Approximately 40% of trademark applications in China are rejected, but most rejections are preventable. Understanding the common rejection reasons helps you avoid costly mistakes and increases your chances of successful registration. This guide explains the top rejection reasons and how to avoid them.

❌ Top 10 Reasons for Trademark Rejection in China

1. Similarity to Existing Trademarks

The Problem:

  • ✓Your mark is too similar to an existing registered trademark
  • ✓CNIPA compares visual, phonetic, and conceptual similarity
  • ✓Even partial similarity can cause rejection
  • ✓Similar marks in related classes also problematic

Common Scenarios:

  • ✓Similar word marks (e.g., "Apple" vs. "Applle")
  • ✓Similar design elements
  • ✓Similar pronunciation in Chinese
  • ✓Similar meaning or concept

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Conduct comprehensive trademark search
  • ✓Search across multiple classes
  • ✓Consider Chinese translations and variations
  • ✓Use professional search services

RTMCN's Solution:

  • ✓Comprehensive search across CNIPA database
  • ✓Analysis of similar marks
  • ✓Risk assessment and recommendations
  • ✓Strategic modification suggestions

2. Lack of Distinctiveness

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark is too generic or descriptive
  • ✓Cannot distinguish your goods/services
  • ✓Common words or phrases
  • ✓Industry-standard terms

Examples of Non-Distinctive Marks:

  • ✓"Best Quality" for any product
  • ✓"Fast Delivery" for shipping services
  • ✓"Cheap Prices" for retail
  • ✓Generic product names

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Create unique, invented words
  • ✓Combine distinctive elements
  • ✓Use arbitrary or fanciful marks
  • ✓Avoid descriptive terms

RTMCN's Approach:

  • ✓Distinctiveness analysis
  • ✓Creative mark development
  • ✓Strategic recommendations
  • ✓Alternative mark suggestions

3. Prohibited Elements

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark contains prohibited content
  • ✓Violates Chinese laws or regulations
  • ✓Offensive or immoral content
  • ✓Government symbols or names

Prohibited Elements Include:

  • ✓National flags, emblems, or anthems
  • ✓Government or military symbols
  • ✓International organization names
  • ✓Offensive or immoral content
  • ✓Geographic names (with exceptions)

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Review CNIPA prohibited list
  • ✓Avoid government-related elements
  • ✓Ensure mark is appropriate
  • ✓Consult with experts

RTMCN's Guidance:

  • ✓Prohibited element screening
  • ✓Legal compliance check
  • ✓Modification recommendations
  • ✓Risk assessment

4. Incorrect Classification

The Problem:

  • ✓Wrong class selected for goods/services
  • ✓Incomplete classification
  • ✓Missing related items
  • ✓Incorrect description

Common Mistakes:

  • ✓Selecting too broad class
  • ✓Missing key goods/services
  • ✓Using incorrect terminology
  • ✓Incomplete item description

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Use CNIPA classification guide
  • ✓Consult with classification experts
  • ✓Include all relevant items
  • ✓Use standard terminology

RTMCN's Expertise:

  • ✓Accurate classification analysis
  • ✓Comprehensive item selection
  • ✓Standard terminology usage
  • ✓Strategic planning

5. Prior Rights Conflicts

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark conflicts with prior rights
  • ✓Copyright, patent, or personal name conflicts
  • ✓Trade name or business name conflicts
  • ✓Domain name conflicts

Types of Prior Rights:

  • ✓Copyrighted works
  • ✓Patented designs
  • ✓Personal names
  • ✓Trade names
  • ✓Domain names

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Search for prior rights
  • ✓Consider all types of conflicts
  • ✓Obtain necessary permissions
  • ✓Modify mark if needed

RTMCN's Service:

  • ✓Prior rights search
  • ✓Conflict analysis
  • ✓Resolution strategies
  • ✓Legal guidance

6. Bad Faith Filing

The Problem:

  • ✓Application filed in bad faith
  • ✓Intent to squat on others' marks
  • ✓Copying famous brands
  • ✓Filing without intent to use

Signs of Bad Faith:

  • ✓Filing multiple famous marks
  • ✓No business relationship
  • ✓Intent to sell back
  • ✓Pattern of squatting

How to Avoid:

  • ✓File marks you actually use
  • ✓Avoid copying famous brands
  • ✓Have legitimate business purpose
  • ✓Document intent to use

RTMCN's Prevention:

  • ✓Good faith documentation
  • ✓Business purpose verification
  • ✓Legitimate use planning
  • ✓Legal compliance

7. Generic or Descriptive Terms

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark is generic term for goods/services
  • ✓Common dictionary words
  • ✓Industry-standard terminology
  • ✓Geographic descriptions

Examples:

  • ✓"Computer" for computers
  • ✓"Shanghai" for Shanghai-based services
  • ✓"Organic" for organic products
  • ✓"Premium" for luxury goods

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Create distinctive combinations
  • ✓Add distinctive elements
  • ✓Use arbitrary terms
  • ✓Consider foreign words

RTMCN's Strategy:

  • ✓Distinctiveness enhancement
  • ✓Combination suggestions
  • ✓Alternative mark development
  • ✓Strategic positioning

8. Deceptive or Misleading Marks

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark deceives or misleads consumers
  • ✓False claims about quality or origin
  • ✓Misleading descriptions
  • ✓Exaggerated claims

Examples:

  • ✓"100% Natural" for synthetic products
  • ✓"Made in Italy" for Chinese-made goods
  • ✓"Award-Winning" without awards
  • ✓"Best in World" claims

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Ensure mark is truthful
  • ✓Avoid exaggerated claims
  • ✓Verify all statements
  • ✓Be accurate in descriptions

RTMCN's Review:

  • ✓Truthfulness verification
  • ✓Claim validation
  • ✓Compliance check
  • ✓Modification suggestions

9. Improper Mark Format

The Problem:

  • ✓Mark format doesn't meet CNIPA requirements
  • ✓Poor quality specimen
  • ✓Incorrect dimensions
  • ✓Missing elements

Format Requirements:

  • ✓Clear, legible design
  • ✓Proper dimensions (minimum 5cm × 5cm)
  • ✓Black and white or color specification
  • ✓Proper file format

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Follow CNIPA format guidelines
  • ✓Use professional design services
  • ✓Ensure high-quality specimen
  • ✓Meet all technical requirements

RTMCN's Support:

  • ✓Format compliance check
  • ✓Design optimization
  • ✓Specimen preparation
  • ✓Technical guidance

10. Incomplete Documentation

The Problem:

  • ✓Missing required documents
  • ✓Incomplete application form
  • ✓Missing signatures or seals
  • ✓Incorrect information

Required Documents:

  • ✓Application form
  • ✓Trademark specimen
  • ✓Priority document (if claiming)
  • ✓Power of attorney
  • ✓Business license or ID

How to Avoid:

  • ✓Use comprehensive checklist
  • ✓Double-check all documents
  • ✓Ensure completeness
  • ✓Professional preparation

RTMCN's Service:

  • ✓Document preparation
  • ✓Completeness verification
  • ✓Quality control
  • ✓Submission management

❌ How to Avoid Trademark Rejection

Step 1: Comprehensive Search

What to Search:

  • ✓CNIPA trademark database
  • ✓Similar marks in related classes
  • ✓Chinese translations and variations
  • ✓Prior rights and conflicts

Search Methods:

  • ✓Online CNIPA database
  • ✓Professional search services
  • ✓Agent-assisted search
  • ✓International databases

RTMCN's Search:

  • ✓Comprehensive database coverage
  • ✓Multiple search strategies
  • ✓Risk assessment report
  • ✓Strategic recommendations

Step 2: Professional Classification

Classification Strategy:

  • ✓Use CNIPA classification guide
  • ✓Include all relevant items
  • ✓Consider related classes
  • ✓Plan for future expansion

Common Mistakes:

  • ✓Too narrow classification
  • ✓Missing key items
  • ✓Incorrect terminology
  • ✓Incomplete coverage

RTMCN's Expertise:

  • ✓Accurate classification
  • ✓Comprehensive coverage
  • ✓Strategic planning
  • ✓Future-proof strategy

Step 3: Distinctiveness Enhancement

Enhancement Methods:

  • ✓Add distinctive elements
  • ✓Create unique combinations
  • ✓Use arbitrary terms
  • ✓Avoid descriptive language

Examples:

  • ✓"TechSolutions" → "TechNova"
  • ✓"FastDelivery" → "QuickShip"
  • ✓"BestQuality" → "PrimeCraft"
  • ✓"ChinaTrade" → "SinoBridge"

RTMCN's Approach:

  • ✓Creative mark development
  • ✓Distinctiveness analysis
  • ✓Alternative suggestions
  • ✓Strategic positioning

Step 4: Legal Compliance Check

Compliance Areas:

  • ✓Prohibited elements
  • ✓Prior rights
  • ✓Good faith requirements
  • ✓Documentation completeness

Verification Process:

  • ✓Legal review
  • ✓Compliance assessment
  • ✓Risk identification
  • ✓Modification recommendations

RTMCN's Service:

  • ✓Comprehensive compliance check
  • ✓Legal guidance
  • ✓Risk mitigation
  • ✓Documentation support

Step 5: Professional Preparation

Preparation Steps:

  • ✓Complete application form
  • ✓High-quality specimen
  • ✓All required documents
  • ✓Proper signatures and seals

Quality Control:

  • ✓Document review
  • ✓Completeness check
  • ✓Accuracy verification
  • ✓Professional presentation

RTMCN's Support:

  • ✓Document preparation
  • ✓Quality assurance
  • ✓Submission management
  • ✓Ongoing support

📌 What to Do If Your Trademark Is Rejected

Option 1: Appeal the Decision

Grounds for Appeal:

  • ✓Procedural errors
  • ✓Misinterpretation of facts
  • ✓Incorrect application of law
  • ✓New evidence available

Appeal Process:

  • •Review rejection notice
  • •Identify appeal grounds
  • •Prepare appeal documents
  • •Submit to Trademark Review Board
  • •Wait for decision

Timeline: 9-12 months

RTMCN's Service:

  • ✓Appeal analysis
  • ✓Document preparation
  • ✓Representation
  • ✓Strategic guidance

Option 2: Modify and Refile

When to Consider:

  • ✓Rejection based on correctable issues
  • ✓Mark can be modified
  • ✓Classification can be adjusted
  • ✓Documentation can be improved

Modification Strategy:

  • ✓Address rejection reasons
  • ✓Enhance distinctiveness
  • ✓Improve classification
  • ✓Strengthen documentation

RTMCN's Approach:

  • ✓Rejection analysis
  • ✓Modification strategy
  • ✓Refile preparation
  • ✓Success optimization

Option 3: Negotiate with Prior Rights Holder

When Applicable:

  • ✓Rejection based on similarity to existing mark
  • ✓Prior rights holder is willing to negotiate
  • ✓Coexistence agreement possible
  • ✓Assignment or license available

Negotiation Process:

  • •Identify prior rights holder
  • •Contact for negotiation
  • •Discuss coexistence or assignment
  • •Reach agreement
  • •Submit to CNIPA

RTMCN's Support:

  • ✓Contact facilitation
  • ✓Negotiation assistance
  • ✓Agreement preparation
  • ✓CNIPA submission

Option 4: Choose Alternative Mark

When to Consider:

  • ✓Rejection reasons are fundamental
  • ✓Mark cannot be modified sufficiently
  • ✓Prior rights conflict is insurmountable
  • ✓Time and cost constraints

Alternative Strategy:

  • ✓Develop new mark
  • ✓Conduct fresh search
  • ✓Ensure distinctiveness
  • ✓File new application

RTMCN's Service:

  • ✓Alternative mark development
  • ✓Comprehensive search
  • ✓Strategic planning
  • ✓New application preparation
